annotations.label: Product rule (Leibniz rule)

anchor.snippet: the product rule (or Leibniz rule

annotations.note: The classical product rule for `deriv` is `deriv_mul` in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Mul`, with `HasDerivAt`/`HasStrictDerivAt`/`fderiv` variants alongside.

annotations.label: Leibniz's infinitesimal derivation

anchor.snippet: Let u and v be functions. Then d(uv) is the same thing as the difference between two successive uv

annotations.note: This is a historical motivational sketch using infinitesimal differences, not a formal Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Differential form via negligible du·dv

anchor.snippet: Since the term du · dv is "negligible"

annotations.note: Heuristic Leibnizian derivation discarding second-order infinitesimals; no Mathlib decl corresponds to this informal manipulation.

annotations.label: Differentiating a product example

annotations.note: A worked numerical example rather than a theorem; no specific Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Constant multiple rule

anchor.snippet: One special case of the product rule is the constant multiple rule

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `deriv_const_mul` (and `HasDerivAt.const_mul`, `deriv_mul_const`) for the constant-multiple special case.

annotations.label: Derivation of integration by parts and quotient rule

anchor.snippet: The rule for integration by parts is derived from the product rule

annotations.note: The downstream consequences (integration by parts, `deriv_div` in `Deriv.Inv`) are formalized, but the meta-derivation linking them to the product rule is not.

annotations.label: Proof via limit definition

anchor.snippet: suppose that f and g are each differentiable at x . We want to prove that h is differentiable at x

annotations.note: This describes a proof strategy for the product rule; Mathlib's proof of `HasDerivAt.mul` uses bilinear-map machinery rather than this textbook limit argument.

annotations.label: Product rule as case of multivariable chain rule

anchor.snippet: The product rule can be considered a special case of the chain rule for several variables

annotations.note: An expository remark about derivation; no explicit Mathlib lemma asserts product rule as a chain-rule corollary.

annotations.note: Hyperreal-style proof; not present as a Mathlib derivation.

annotations.label: Logarithmic differentiation proof

anchor.snippet: Taking the absolute value of each function and the natural log of both sides

annotations.note: A proof technique; the related identity `logDeriv_mul` exists in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.LogDeriv` but the proof itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Product rule for three factors

anchor.section: Product of more than two factors
anchor.snippet: The product rule can be generalized to products of more than two factors

annotations.note: No three-factor lemma is stated explicitly, but it follows trivially from iterating `deriv_mul`; the n-fold case is via `iteratedDeriv_mul`.

annotations.label: Logarithmic derivative of a product

anchor.section: Product of more than two factors
anchor.snippet: The logarithmic derivative provides a simpler expression of the last form

annotations.note: `logDeriv_mul` in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.LogDeriv` gives `logDeriv (f*g) = logDeriv f + logDeriv g`.

annotations.label: General Leibniz rule (n-th derivative)

anchor.snippet: It can also be generalized to the general Leibniz rule for the n th derivative of a product of two factors

annotations.note: `iteratedDeriv_mul` states the binomial-coefficient Leibniz formula for the n-th derivative of a product.

annotations.label: n-th derivative at a specific point

anchor.snippet: Applied at a specific point x , the above formula gives

annotations.note: Mathlib's `iteratedDeriv_mul` is already pointwise at `x`, matching the article's pointwise restatement.

annotations.label: n-th derivative of arbitrary number of factors

anchor.snippet: for the n th derivative of an arbitrary number of factors

annotations.note: No multinomial Leibniz formula for the n-th derivative of a finite product of factors was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Higher partial derivatives formula

anchor.snippet: For partial derivatives , we have

annotations.note: Multi-index Leibniz formula for mixed higher partial derivatives is not present as a dedicated lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Product rule for continuous bilinear operator on Banach spaces

anchor.snippet: Suppose X , Y , and Z are Banach spaces

annotations.note: `IsBoundedBilinearMap.hasFDerivAt` (and `.fderiv`) give the derivative of any bounded bilinear map between Banach spaces.

annotations.label: Product rule for scalar multiplication of vector functions

anchor.snippet: For scalar multiplication

annotations.note: `HasDerivAt.smul`/`deriv_smul` in `Deriv.Mul` (and `HasFDerivAt.smul` in `FDeriv.Mul`) give the product rule for scalar multiplication.

annotations.label: Product rule for dot product

anchor.snippet: For dot product

annotations.note: `HasFDerivAt.inner`, `HasDerivAt.inner`, and `fderiv_inner_apply` provide the product rule for inner/dot products.

annotations.label: Product rule for cross product

anchor.snippet: For cross product of vector functions

annotations.note: No derivative-of-cross-product lemma was found; `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.CrossProduct` defines the operation but no calculus-side product rule.

annotations.label: Product rule with the gradient

anchor.snippet: if f and g are scalar fields then there is a product rule with the gradient

annotations.note: `fderiv_mul` gives the Fréchet-derivative product rule, which specializes to ∇(fg) = f∇g + g∇f, but no dedicated `gradient_mul` lemma was found in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Gradient.Basic`.

annotations.label: Product rule for continuous bilinear operations

anchor.snippet: Such a rule will hold for any continuous bilinear product operation

annotations.note: Same `IsBoundedBilinearMap.hasFDerivAt` covers the general continuous-bilinear case.

annotations.label: Derivation (abstract algebra)

anchor.section: Derivations in abstract algebra and differential geometry
anchor.snippet: the product rule is the defining property of a derivation

annotations.note: The `Derivation` structure in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.Basic` axiomatises Leibniz via `leibniz' : D (a*b) = a • D b + b • D a`.

annotations.label: Tangent vector as derivation; graded Leibniz rule

anchor.section: Derivations in abstract algebra and differential geometry
anchor.snippet: a tangent vector to a manifold M at a point p may be defined abstractly as an operator

annotations.note: `PointDerivation` formalizes tangent vectors as derivations on smooth functions, but the graded-Leibniz rule for differential forms is not directly captured by this declaration.

annotations.label: Power rule for positive integer n

anchor.snippet: Among the applications of the product rule is a proof that

annotations.note: `deriv_pow` and `HasDerivAt.pow` in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Deriv.Pow` give d/dx xⁿ = n·xⁿ⁻¹ for positive integer n.
